description: The camera is facing a bright window, causing the window to glow white and making the room interior look like a dark silhouette.
action: Tap the darkest part of the screen to adjust exposure, or shoot from an angle where the window is on your left/right, not straight ahead.
photo_tip: Tap the dark part of your phone screen to lock focus and raise the exposure before pressing the shutter button.

description: The room has a mix of blue daylight bulbs and yellow warm bulbs turned on simultaneously, creating muddy colors.
action: Standardize all bulbs to warm white (2700K-3000K) for a cohesive, cozy look.
photo_tip: Turn off your phone's auto-flash and HDR to prevent the camera from over-processing the conflicting light colors.

description: A harsh white reflection from the camera flash is visible in mirrors, windows, or TV screens.
action: Turn off the flash completely. Rely on natural light and all interior room lights.
photo_tip: Always force the flash OFF in camera settings when shooting interiors with reflective surfaces like glass or mirrors.

description: Important features like the bed or a fireplace are cut off at the edges of the photo.
action: Use the wide-angle lens (0.5x) but step back. Ensure the main furniture piece is fully within the frame.
photo_tip: Stand literally in the corner or doorway of the room and use the 0.5x wide-angle setting on your phone to capture everything.

description: The corners of the room are hidden in deep shadows, making the space look tiny and gloomy.
action: Place an accent light or floor lamp in the darkest corner to bounce light off the walls and visually expand the room.
photo_tip: Wait for 'Golden Hour' (shortly after sunrise or before sunset) so natural sunlight reaches deeper into the room.

description: Only one bedside lamp is turned on, creating an unbalanced and sloppy look.
action: Turn on BOTH bedside lamps to create perfect symmetry, ensuring they use the exact same wattage and color.
photo_tip: Stand exactly at the center foot of the bed and hold the camera perfectly level at chest height to emphasize symmetry.

description: The TV is left on, casting a blue, unnatural glow over the room and distracting from the interior design.
action: Turn off the TV so it is a clean black rectangle, or display a static, high-resolution artwork image.
photo_tip: If the TV is off and reflecting the window like a mirror, change your angle slightly so the window reflection disappears from the screen.

description: The bathroom is lit only by a single dim ceiling light, casting dark shadows over the vanity area.
action: Ensure all vanity and mirror lights are turned on. Bathroom lighting should be bright to feel clean.
photo_tip: Shoot from the doorway using the wide-angle lens. Avoid capturing your own reflection in the bathroom mirror.

description: A bare bulb on a ceiling fan is casting harsh, rotating, or spider-like shadows across the ceiling.
action: Turn off the ceiling fan light. Rely instead on floor lamps and natural light for more flattering illumination.
photo_tip: Ensure the ceiling fan blades are completely stopped before taking the photo to avoid blurry motion in the shot.

description: The photo of the balcony or exterior is taken in the middle of a cloudy day or in complete darkness.
action: Shoot exterior spaces during 'Twilight' (just after sunset) with all interior lights turned on so they glow warmly through the windows.
photo_tip: Use 'Night Mode' on your phone and hold it very still for 3 seconds to capture twilight architectural shots clearly.

description: A double/queen bed has one side pushed flat against a wall, looking like a dorm room.
action: Center the bed on the main wall. Leave at least 2 feet of walking space on both sides with matching nightstands.
photo_tip: Take the photo straight-on from the foot of the bed to clearly show the spacing on both left and right sides.

description: An area rug is too small and sits in the middle of the floor without touching any furniture legs.
action: Use a larger rug. The front legs of the sofa or the bottom two-thirds of the bed must rest on the rug to anchor the space.
photo_tip: Aim the camera slightly downward to show how the furniture legs interact with the edges of the rug.

description: The back of a sofa or a large chair is blocking the natural entrance or walkway of the room.
action: Rearrange furniture to open the flow. Pull items away from the entryway so the room feels welcoming immediately.
photo_tip: Stand in the hallway or doorway looking into the room. Shoot from chest height to show the obstruction in the walking path.

description: All furniture is pushed tightly against the walls, leaving a massive, awkward empty space in the middle.
action: Pull the sofa and chairs closer together to create an intimate conversation area. Floating furniture makes rooms feel larger.
photo_tip: Shoot from an elevated angle (like standing on a small stool) to show the excessive floor space in the center.

description: Dining chairs are pushed so close to a wall that a guest cannot pull them out to sit.
action: Ensure 36 inches of clearance between the table edge and walls. If too tight, remove a chair or use a smaller round table.
photo_tip: Include both the edge of the dining table and the nearby wall in the frame to clearly demonstrate the lack of clearance.

description: A massive nightstand sits on one side of the bed and a tiny, flimsy table on the other.
action: Symmetry sells. Ensure both nightstands are roughly the same visual weight and height to frame the bed perfectly.
photo_tip: Take a horizontal (landscape) photo capturing the bed and both nightstands in a single frame to highlight the imbalance.

description: The coffee table is placed out of reach from the sofa, making it look decorative rather than functional.
action: Place the coffee table exactly 16 to 18 inches away from the edge of the sofa for perfect ergonomic reach.
photo_tip: Shoot from a side-profile angle showing the gap between the sofa edge and the coffee table.

description: A tall piece of furniture, like a bookshelf or a high headboard, is blocking a window and cutting off natural light.
action: Move tall furniture to solid walls. Never block windows, as natural light is the most valuable asset in a photo.
photo_tip: Ensure the window is visible in the shot, and tap on the window frame on your screen so the camera balances the light.

description: The room is cluttered with multiple small chairs, tiny tables, and bean bags instead of fewer, substantial pieces.
action: Consolidate furniture. Replace three small seating items with one solid armchair or loveseat to reduce visual noise.
photo_tip: Take a wide shot from the corner of the room to capture the full extent of the furniture clutter.

description: A small TV is placed on a massive blank wall with no console table underneath it to ground it.
action: Place a media console under the TV that is at least 25% wider than the TV itself to visually anchor the setup.
photo_tip: Frame the shot so the entire wall from floor to ceiling is visible, emphasizing how the TV looks 'lost' on the blank space.

description: A tangled mess of black power cords or HDMI cables hangs from the TV or runs across the floor.
action: Zip-tie cables together and hide them behind furniture legs, or install a cheap wall cable concealer.
photo_tip: Focus directly on the messy cables and ensure the area is well-lit so the contrast of black cables on the wall/floor is clear.

description: Family photos, mail, used sponges, or toothbrushes are visible on counters.
action: Depersonalize completely. Remove all personal items to create a clean, hotel-like environment.
photo_tip: Take a medium-distance shot of the kitchen or bathroom counter to show how the personal items break the clean lines.

description: The bed looks slept in, the duvet is flat, and there are only two sleeping pillows.
action: Iron the duvet. Fold the top back to show crisp sheets, and use at least 4 propped-up sleeping pillows plus 1-2 decorative throw pillows.
photo_tip: Shoot at a 45-degree angle from the foot of the bed. This angle highlights the lack of volume and layers in the bedding.

description: The curtain rod is resting directly on the top window frame, making the ceiling look low.
action: Hang the curtain rod 4-6 inches above the window frame and let the fabric touch the floor to add height to the room.
photo_tip: Hold the phone perfectly vertical and frame the shot to include both the top of the window and the ceiling above it.

description: Large blank white walls with zero artwork or mirrors make the room feel sterile.
action: Hang a large canvas or framed print above the bed or sofa, with the center of the art at eye level.
photo_tip: Stand back and center the empty wall in your frame to emphasize the lack of decor.

description: The room looks one-dimensional because all materials are flat (e.g., plain cotton) with no cozy textures.
action: Add layered textures. Throw a chunky knit blanket over the bed or sofa, and add a velvet or linen throw pillow.
photo_tip: Get closer to the sofa or bed. Textures are best captured in medium shots, not extreme wide angles.

description: Coffee tables or nightstands are covered in too many small decorative items.
action: Apply the 'Rule of Three'. Leave a maximum of three curated items (e.g., a plant, a book, a lamp) on any flat surface.
photo_tip: Take a top-down ('bird's eye view') photo of the coffee table to clearly show the clutter footprint.

description: Windows have no blinds, shades, or curtains, making the room feel unfinished and lacking privacy.
action: Install simple, floor-length sheer curtains to soften the light and make the room look professionally styled.
photo_tip: Take the photo during the day, showing the harsh light entering the room due to the lack of window treatments.

description: The room design lacks ambient lighting options, relying solely on a bright ceiling fixture.
action: Add table lamps or a floor lamp. Layered lighting is essential for creating a luxurious, welcoming atmosphere.
photo_tip: Turn off the flash. Tap your screen to lower the exposure so the camera captures how harsh the ceiling light shadows are.

description: Kitchen or bathroom cabinets have cheap, old, or mismatched plastic handles.
action: Swap out old cabinet pulls for modern matte black or brushed brass hardware. It is a cheap fix that elevates the whole room.
photo_tip: Take a close-up, macro shot of the handle against the cabinet door, making sure it is in sharp focus.

description: Gutters are sagging, pulling away from the wood fascia, or missing completely.
action: Re-secure gutters using heavy-duty hidden hangers with screws. Clean out debris causing excess weight.
photo_tip: Stand directly under the gutter and shoot upwards, or zoom in from a distance to show the exact gap between the gutter and the roof.

description: The soil or concrete around the house slopes downward toward the foundation, pooling water.
action: Regrade the soil to drop 6 inches over the first 10 feet away from the house to prevent basement flooding.
photo_tip: Crouch down low to the ground and take a profile shot of the yard sloping towards the wall to show the negative angle.

description: Asphalt roof shingles are missing, cracked, or curling upward at the edges.
action: The roof has reached the end of its lifespan. Hire a roofer to patch missing areas or replace the roof.
photo_tip: Use your phone's optical zoom (e.g., 3x or 5x) from the ground to safely photograph roof damage without using a ladder.

description: Wood or composite siding near the ground is soft, crumbling, or peeling heavily.
action: Replace rotted boards. Ensure there is at least 6 inches of clearance between the bottom of the siding and the soil.
photo_tip: Get close and ensure the shot includes both the rotted wood and the ground underneath it to show moisture contact.

description: The metal base where the chimney meets the roof is rusted, lifted, or heavily tarred.
action: Have a professional install new custom-bent metal step flashing to prevent water intrusion into the attic.
photo_tip: Zoom in on the specific joint where the metal meets the brick. Ensure the area isn't in deep shadow.

description: Large sections of paint are bubbling, flaking, or peeling off the exterior siding or trim.
action: Scrape away loose paint, apply a high-quality exterior primer to bare wood, and repaint to prevent moisture rot.
photo_tip: Take a medium-distance shot that shows the scale of the peeling, rather than just one microscopic flake.

description: Thick green moss or algae is growing on the roof shingles, especially on shaded sections.
action: Apply a zinc or copper-based moss killer. Do not power-wash the roof, as it destroys the shingle granules.
photo_tip: Photograph the affected area on an overcast day if possible to avoid harsh sun glare hiding the green moss.

description: The mortar joints between the bricks on the chimney are missing, sandy, or crumbling away.
action: Hire a mason to 'tuckpoint' the chimney. Leaving it exposes the structure to water damage and collapse.
photo_tip: Use optical zoom. Focus specifically on the grey lines between the bricks to capture the depth of the missing mortar.

description: The exterior caulk around window frames is cracked, pulling away, or completely missing.
action: Remove the old caulk and apply a fresh bead of exterior-grade polyurethane or silicone caulk to stop drafts and leaks.
photo_tip: Take a close-up macro shot of the corner of the window frame where gaps are most common. Tap the gap to focus.

description: Large tree branches are resting on the roof or hanging directly over the chimney.
action: Trim branches back at least 6 feet from the roof to prevent physical damage during storms and keep pests away.
photo_tip: Stand back far enough to capture the whole house and the tree in the same frame to show how they intersect.

description: Black or brown burn marks are visible on the plastic faceplate of an outlet.
action: Turn off the breaker immediately. This is a severe fire hazard indicating arcing. Call an electrician.
photo_tip: Turn ON your camera flash to clearly illuminate the black scorch marks against the wall. Do NOT touch the outlet.

description: Standard outlets (without test/reset buttons) are located within 6 feet of a sink or bathtub.
action: Upgrade to GFCI receptacles to protect against fatal electric shocks near water sources.
photo_tip: Frame the photo to include both the standard outlet AND the nearby sink/faucet to prove the distance.

description: Wires joined with plastic nuts are hanging outside of a covered junction box.
action: All wire connections must be enclosed in a secured plastic or metal junction box to contain potential sparks.
photo_tip: Take a clear shot of the exposed wire nuts. Use flash if in a dark attic or basement, but keep your hands away.

description: Outlet covers and the actual plug slots are heavily coated in interior wall paint.
action: Replace the painted receptacles and covers. Paint in the slots can cause poor connections and overheating.
photo_tip: Get close enough so the camera captures the paint actually clogging the small vertical slots of the plug.

description: Multiple high-draw appliances are plugged into a single cheap extension cord or power strip.
action: Unplug heavy appliances. Install permanent wall outlets. Extension cords are for temporary use only and can melt.
photo_tip: Capture the entire power strip showing all the tangled plugs. Make sure the room is well lit.

description: The home has older two-prong outlets instead of modern three-prong grounded outlets.
action: This means the system lacks an equipment ground. Have an electrician install GFCI outlets or rewire the circuits for modern safety.
photo_tip: Take a straight-on, close-up shot of the outlet faceplate to clearly show it lacks the third bottom hole.

description: Inside the electrical panel, two separate wires are crammed into a single circuit breaker slot.
action: This is a fire hazard due to loose connections. An electrician must add a new breaker or use a pigtail connection if permitted.
photo_tip: Use your phone's macro lens or zoom in close inside the panel box. Ensure good lighting so the two distinct wires are visible.

description: Indoor-rated NM (Romex) wiring is running along the outside of the house without a protective conduit.
action: Outdoor wiring must be UF-rated (Underground Feeder) or fully enclosed in weather-proof PVC or metal conduit to prevent UV and physical damage.
photo_tip: Include a piece of the exterior wall (like brick or siding) in the shot so it's obvious the wire is outdoors.

description: Lights flicker or dim noticeably when a major appliance (like a vacuum or AC) turns on.
action: This indicates an overloaded circuit or loose neutral connection. Have an electrician evaluate the panel's load capacity.
photo_tip: For flickering issues, take a short 3-second VIDEO instead of a photo, capturing the light bulb dimming.

description: A dimmer switch or standard light switch feels warm or hot to the touch during normal use.
action: A hot switch indicates it is failing or handling too much wattage. Turn it off and replace the switch immediately.
photo_tip: While a photo can't show heat, take a clear shot of the switch and any visible discoloration or melting on the plastic.

description: Brown staining or rotting flooring around the base of the toilet.
action: The wax ring has failed. Remove the toilet, repair rotted subfloor, and install a new extra-thick wax ring.
photo_tip: Take a close-up shot at floor level showing where the porcelain base meets the tile or linoleum.

description: Heavy white or green crusty buildup on the P-trap pipes under a sink.
action: This indicates a slow micro-leak. Disassemble the P-trap, replace the rubber slip-joint washers, and hand-tighten.
photo_tip: Turn ON your camera flash. Clear out any cleaning bottles blocking the view before taking the picture.

description: Rust flakes or active water weeping around the bottom seam of a hot water tank.
action: The internal tank lining has failed. Replace the water heater immediately before it bursts and floods the room.
photo_tip: Crouch down and shoot the exact bottom edge of the tank where it meets the floor pan, using flash to highlight rust.

description: Older silver pipes show rusty, bubbly blisters on the outside.
action: The pipes are rusting from the inside out and are paper-thin. Budget for a home re-pipe with PEX or copper.
photo_tip: Tap on the pipe on your screen to focus. Blisters are small, so macro mode or zooming in is essential here.

description: A faucet has heavy white calcium buildup at the tip and is actively dripping water.
action: Replace the internal cartridge or O-rings to stop the drip, and soak the aerator in white vinegar to remove minerals.
photo_tip: Use a fast shutter speed or burst mode to catch the actual water droplet mid-air falling from the faucet.

description: Water is pooling in the sink or tub and not draining, often with visible hair or debris in the crosshairs of the drain.
action: Remove the stopper and clear the blockage using a plastic drain snake. Avoid harsh liquid drain cleaners as they damage older pipes.
photo_tip: Shoot directly down into the sink or tub drain to clearly show the standing water and any visible clogs.

description: Copper or PVC water supply pipes in an unheated garage, basement, or crawlspace have no foam insulation.
action: Wrap exposed pipes with foam pipe insulation tubes and secure with zip ties to prevent freezing and bursting during winter.
photo_tip: Include a wide enough angle to show the surrounding unfinished basement/garage environment, proving it's an unheated space.

description: The outdoor water spigot is actively dripping or has a large ice pillar formed under it in winter.
action: Replace the internal stem washer. If it's a freeze-proof bib, ensure the hose is disconnected in winter so water can drain out.
photo_tip: Shoot from an angle where the dripping water is silhouetted against a darker background so the leak is visible.

description: The sump pump pit is overflowing with water, or the discharge pipe outside is buried under dirt.
action: Test the sump pump float switch immediately. Ensure the exterior discharge pipe directs water at least 6 feet away from the foundation.
photo_tip: Take a photo straight down into the sump pit, using flash to pierce through the dark water and show the pump.

description: With all faucets off, the small red triangle or dial on the water meter is still spinning.
action: You have a hidden active leak, likely a running toilet or an underground pipe break. Shut off the main water valve and investigate.
photo_tip: Clean the glass of the meter first if it's dusty. Take a short video clip instead of a photo to show the dial spinning.

description: Thick white ice or frost covers the copper refrigerant lines or indoor coils.
action: Turn the AC off immediately to melt the ice. Check for a completely clogged air filter or call a tech for low refrigerant.
photo_tip: Ensure good lighting. Ice can look like white glare on camera, so adjust exposure down slightly to capture the frost texture.

description: The return air filter is completely gray, bent from suction, or covered in a thick layer of dust and hair.
action: Replace the filter with a new MERV 8 filter immediately to restore airflow and prevent blower motor burnout.
photo_tip: Pull the filter out halfway from its slot before taking the photo so the contrast between the clean slot and dirty filter is obvious.

description: Standing water or heavy rust in the metal secondary pan under the attic unit.
action: The primary condensate line is clogged. Clear the PVC pipe with a wet vacuum from the outside to prevent ceiling leaks.
photo_tip: Attics are dark. Use your strongest flashlight to illuminate the pan while snapping the photo, rather than relying just on phone flash.

description: Rugs, sofas, or bookcases are placed directly over floor supply registers.
action: Move furniture to allow at least 12 inches of clearance over vents so the room can heat and cool efficiently.
photo_tip: Take the photo from a high angle looking down to show how the furniture physically overlaps the metal floor vent.

description: Foil ducting in the attic or crawlspace is flattened, torn open, or completely disconnected.
action: Replace or reconnect the damaged duct sections using metallic foil tape and mastic sealant to stop pumping air into the attic.
photo_tip: Take a wide shot to show the entire run of the duct, making it easier to see the 'kink' or crush point.

description: The outdoor AC unit is leaning heavily to one side because the plastic or concrete pad has sunk into the dirt.
action: Level the pad using gravel or sand underneath. An unlevel unit prevents proper oil lubrication inside the compressor, leading to premature failure.
photo_tip: Step back about 10 feet. Line up the edge of the AC unit with a straight vertical line in the background (like the house corner) to highlight the tilt.

description: The metal fins on the outside of the outdoor AC unit are completely blocked by grass clippings, mud, or dead leaves.
action: Turn off power to the unit and gently wash the exterior fins with a garden hose (low pressure) to restore heat transfer efficiency.
photo_tip: Get very close to the metal fins. Tap your screen to focus directly on the dirt/leaves trapped in the grid.

description: The black foam insulation on the larger copper pipe running to the outdoor AC unit is missing, cracked, or degraded by the sun.
action: Wrap the exposed copper pipe with new foam insulation and secure with zip ties. This prevents condensation and efficiency loss.
photo_tip: Focus closely on the bare copper pipe where the black foam has stripped away. Ensure it's in sharp focus.

description: Black powdery soot is visible around the front covers or exhaust vents of a gas furnace.
action: Shut off the furnace immediately. This indicates poor combustion and a high risk of Carbon Monoxide poisoning. Call a licensed HVAC technician.
photo_tip: Wipe a clean white tissue over the soot and photograph the tissue next to the furnace to clearly show the black residue.

description: The metal exhaust pipe above the gas water heater or furnace has heavy rust, holes, or white powdery corrosion at the joints.
action: Replace the rusted flue pipe sections. Any holes can allow lethal Carbon Monoxide gas to vent directly into the living space.
photo_tip: Use a flashlight to light up the dark joints of the silver flue pipe where the white corrosion usually hides.

description: Condensation or a milky white haze is trapped permanently between two panes of window glass.
action: The thermal seal has failed. It's cosmetic but lowers energy efficiency. A glazier can replace just the glass sash.
photo_tip: Shoot from an angle, not straight on. The glare from the angle will highlight the condensation trapped inside the glass.

description: The handrail wiggles or the wall brackets are pulling out of the drywall.
action: Major fall hazard. Re-anchor the brackets using 3-inch wood screws directly into the structural wall studs.
photo_tip: Take a close-up of the metal bracket specifically showing where it meets the drywall, highlighting any gaps.

description: Yellow or brown circular stains on the drywall ceiling.
action: Find the active leak above (roof or plumbing). Fix the source, then paint the ceiling with a stain-blocking primer.
photo_tip: Turn off your flash. Flash can bounce off the white ceiling paint and wash out the yellow water stain.

description: Dark spotty growth on silicone caulk or grout lines in the bathroom.
action: Scrub with bleach. Ensure the exhaust fan works and runs for 30 minutes after showers to vent humidity.
photo_tip: Use your phone's macro mode. Ensure the bathroom is fully lit so the black spots contrast against the white caulk.

description: Small circular bumps or cracks where screw heads are pushing through the paint.
action: Cosmetic issue from wood framing shrinking. Drive a new screw nearby, patch the old hole with spackle, and paint.
photo_tip: Hold a flashlight flat against the wall, pointing across the surface. This casts a shadow on the small bumps, making them pop out on camera.

description: The floor has a noticeable dip, or round objects naturally roll to one side of the room.
action: Could indicate failing floor joists or foundation settling. Inspect the basement/crawlspace directly below this area for structural damage.
photo_tip: Place a round object (like a ball) on the floor and take a video of it rolling, or place a level tool on the floor and photograph the bubble.

description: An interior door hits the top corner of the doorframe and won't close properly, with visible scuff marks on the paint.
action: Often caused by foundation settling. Try tightening the top hinge screws first. If it persists, the door may need to be planed or the hinges adjusted.
photo_tip: Close the door as much as possible and take a close-up of the exact corner where the wood is scraping the frame.

description: Paint on the bathroom ceiling or upper walls is peeling in large strips due to heavy moisture.
action: Scrape loose paint, apply a mold-resistant primer, and repaint with a semi-gloss finish. Upgrade the bathroom exhaust fan to handle the humidity.
photo_tip: Take a medium shot that shows both the peeling ceiling and the shower head below it to establish context.

description: Ceramic floor tiles have long, continuous cracks running through multiple tiles in a straight line.
action: This indicates movement or a crack in the concrete slab underneath. The underlying crack must be treated with an isolation membrane before replacing tiles.
photo_tip: Place a coin next to the crack for scale, and shoot directly downwards from above.

description: Wide, visible gaps between solid hardwood floor planks, mostly appearing in the winter.
action: Wood shrinks in low humidity. Install a whole-house humidifier to keep indoor humidity around 40-50% to prevent the wood from drying out.
photo_tip: Take a close-up shot from directly above, making sure the dark gaps between the wood planks are in sharp focus.

description: Horizontal cracks in basement walls, often accompanied by the wall bowing inward.
action: Do not DIY. The wall is failing from outside soil pressure. Consult a structural engineer immediately.
photo_tip: Stand back to capture the entire crack length. Hold a tape measure or a standard pen across the crack to show its exact width.

description: A visible dip or 'saddle' shape in the main peak of the roof from the outside.
action: Indicates failing rafters or trusses. Requires professional structural reinforcement.
photo_tip: Stand far back across the street. Align the top of your phone screen with the roofline to make the 'dip' incredibly obvious in the photo.

description: Pencil-thin dirt tubes climbing up concrete foundation walls toward wood framing.
action: Active subterranean termites. Do not scrape tubes. Call pest control for localized treatment immediately.
photo_tip: Use a flashlight and macro focus. These look like thin veins of dirt, so high contrast and sharp focus are required.

description: Thin, vertical hairline cracks in poured concrete foundation walls.
action: Usually normal concrete shrinkage. Monitor for widening over 6 months and seal with polyurethane caulk.
photo_tip: Place a ruler next to the crack. Take one wide shot showing the wall from floor to ceiling, and one close-up of the widest part.

description: Wooden deck support posts are soft, splintering, or sinking directly into the dirt without a concrete footer.
action: Structural failure risk. Replace rotted posts with pressure-treated lumber installed on proper elevated concrete footings.
photo_tip: Get low to the ground. Photograph where the wood post directly touches the soil, as this is where the rot occurs.

description: Large, jagged cracks in the drywall originating from the top corners of doorframes or windows.
action: Sign of foundation settlement. If the crack is wider than 1/8 inch or actively growing, have the foundation inspected.
photo_tip: Frame the shot to include the corner of the doorframe so the origin point of the diagonal crack is visible.

description: The surface of the exterior concrete foundation or driveway is chipping, flaking off, and exposing the rough aggregate underneath.
action: Caused by freeze-thaw cycles and water saturation. Improve drainage away from the concrete and apply a breathable concrete sealer.
photo_tip: Take a top-down shot showing the smooth concrete surface contrasting with the rough, broken area.

description: Looking up from the basement or crawlspace, the wooden floor joists show visible deflection, cracking, or are pulling away from the main beam.
action: Major structural hazard. A contractor must 'sister' new joists alongside the failing ones or install adjustable steel lally columns for support.
photo_tip: Use a strong flashlight. Hold the camera parallel to the joist to capture the curve/bend in the wood.

description: An exterior brick, stone, or timber retaining wall is leaning heavily outward toward the yard.
action: The wall lacks proper drainage (weep holes) and hydrostatic pressure is destroying it. It needs to be excavated, rebuilt, and backfilled with gravel.
photo_tip: Stand at the far end of the wall and shoot down its length (profile view). This angle exaggerates and clearly shows the 'bulge'.

description: A major structural support beam in the basement has large chunks cut out of it by a plumber or electrician to run pipes/wires.
action: Cutting load-bearing beams destroys their integrity. A structural engineer must assess the damage and design a steel or wood reinforcement.
photo_tip: Take a medium shot showing both the cut in the wood beam AND the pipe/wire running through it for context.

description: The metal mesh grease filter under the kitchen exhaust hood is thick with yellow or brown sticky grease.
action: Remove the filter and soak it in boiling water with baking soda and dish soap to dissolve the grease. Prevents grease fires and improves air quality.
photo_tip: Turn on the stove hood light, point the camera straight up, and tap the screen to focus on the metal mesh texture.

description: Thick dust and pet hair are heavily blanketing the condenser coils at the bottom front or back of the refrigerator.
action: Vacuum the coils immediately using a brush attachment. Blocked coils cause the compressor to overheat, drastically shortening the appliance's lifespan.
photo_tip: You will need flash. Pull the fridge out slightly or remove the front kick-plate to get a clear line of sight to the coils.

description: The black heating element at the bottom of the electric oven has white spots, bubbles, or looks deformed.
action: Do not use the oven. The heating element is about to fail and could spark or catch fire. Order a replacement element and install it.
photo_tip: Turn on the oven interior light. Do not use flash, as it will glare off the metal and hide the blisters.

description: Thick, fuzzy grey lint is blocking the exterior exhaust flap of the laundry dryer vent.
action: Clean the vent hose and the exterior exhaust immediately using a vent brush kit. This is one of the leading causes of house fires.
photo_tip: Get close to the exterior vent flap and take a macro shot of the lint buildup blocking the opening.

description: The white plastic casing of the ceiling smoke detector has turned a dark, aged yellow color.
action: Replace the entire unit. Yellowing plastic means the detector is over 10 years old and the internal sensors are no longer reliable.
photo_tip: Ensure the room is well-lit so the camera accurately captures the yellowed tint of the plastic against the white ceiling.

description: One power strip or surge protector is plugged directly into another power strip.
action: Unplug immediately. Daisy-chaining bypasses safety features and overloads the circuit, posing a massive fire hazard.
photo_tip: Take a wide shot that shows both power strips connected to each other to clearly demonstrate the daisy-chain.

description: Small, dark, rice-shaped pellets found in corners of cabinets, pantries, or along baseboards.
action: Wear a mask, spray with a bleach solution, and wipe up. Set snap traps and use steel wool to plug any holes larger than a dime under sinks.
photo_tip: Place a coin next to the droppings for scale, use flash, and shoot straight down to capture the exact size and shape.

description: Small piles of what looks like fine sawdust sitting directly underneath wooden beams or window sills.
action: This is 'frass' (insect waste), indicating carpenter ants are hollowing out your wood. Call a licensed exterminator immediately.
photo_tip: Use macro mode. Include both the sawdust pile and the wooden structure directly above it in the same frame.

description: A small, grey, papery honeycomb structure is being built under the exterior roof eaves or near a doorway.
action: Spray the nest with a dedicated foaming wasp killer at night when the insects are dormant and inside the nest. Remove the nest the next day.
photo_tip: Use your phone's optical zoom (do not get close physically). Frame it to show the nest attached to the building structure.
